summary: Evans argues that obsessing over choosing the 'best' software tool is paralyzing and largely unproductive. She proposes an alternative framework borrowed from capacity planning: only change something if it's too expensive, too hard to operate, or not doing its job. If a system passes those three tests, it doesn't matter whether something theoretically better exists. She suggests that after confirming something will work, you should just pick whatever the team feels best about, since building something that works well enough is already hard enough.
key_points: The 'what is the Best Thing' framework for technical decisions is often paralyzing rather than helpful
Three practical criteria for evaluating systems: cost, operational burden, and whether it does its job
If a system meets all three criteria, searching for something 'better' is wasted effort when real problems exist elsewhere
When multiple options will work, just pick the one people feel best about — team sentiment matters
What counts as 'the system works' can be redefined when someone has a genuinely better idea
money_quotes: I am going to make an argument that 'it literally does not matter what is the best'.
I find these 3 criteria a lot easier to reason about than the 'Choose The Best Thing' framework.
So, it literally does not matter at all if they are the Best System!
Saying that there's a 'tradeoff' doesn't help me at all!
Nobody has ever told me 'you should make technical decision by just figuring out what will work and then just pick whatever is the most popular', but it seems kind of reasonable to me?
I find the idea that 'it doesn't matter what the best thing is' kind of freeing.

Building a system that works well enough within the constraints that you have is already REALLY HARD. So it seems unreasonable to additionally require that that system also be the Best Possible Thing.
key_insight: Instead of searching for the theoretically best tool, evaluate whether your current system is too expensive, too hard to operate, or failing at its job — if it passes all three, move on to real problems.
